title: Activated Gas Transport in Polymer-Grafted Nanoparticle Membranes
abstract: Carbon capture and related gas separation processes are critical tools in our efforts to combat climate change. While polymer membranes are seen as a central construct to achieve these goals, their performance needs further improvement to meet current sustainability goals. It is in this context that membranes composed of polymer-grafted nanoparticles (GNPs) become highly germane. Previous work has shown that gas transport in pure GNP membranes can be strongly enhanced relative to that in the corresponding neat polymer. Additionally, we found that larger gases display greater degrees of permeability enhancement than smaller ones. There is currently no clear understanding of these two underpinning facts, and to address this issue, we have characterized the activation energy driving the permeability of multiple gases in several GNP membranes. Our results show that gases smaller than a critical size display the same activation energy as in the native polymer—any enhancement therefore is associated with changes in local gas/chain dynamics, e.g., the speeding up of polymer dynamics due to the local stretching in a brush conformation. Our results are consistent with the notion that small gases are present in the whole polymer layer. Conversely, larger gases show substantially lower activation energies and, in contrast to the neat polymer, exhibit essentially no dependence on penetrant size. Sorption measurements illustrate that this is a result of the enhanced solubility of larger gases in GNPs, relative to that in the pure polymer, which is reflected in their significantly more favorable enthalpies of solvation. Larger gases thus preferentially sorb into the interstitial spaces in the GNP assembly, thereby resulting in even higher permeability enhancements in the GNP layers relative to smaller gases. GNPs, therefore, provide us with multiple handles to control gas transport, a fact that offers critical insights into their utility for gas separations.
results: **Permeability Measurements.** Temperature-dependent permeability measurements were conducted with a constant-volume, variable-pressure apparatus and plotted as a function of the inverse temperature for three representative gases,  $CO_2$  ( $d_k \approx 3.3$  Å),  $N_2$  ( $d_k \approx 3.6$  Å), and  $CH_4$  ( $d_k \approx 3.8$  Å), in the GNPs with  $c3 \approx 0.42$  chain/nm² grafted with PMA chains with  $M_n \sim 98$  kDa (Figure 2a, data in Supporting Information Figures S2—S4). With the caveat that the data spans less than one order of magnitude in permeability, we fit them to an Arrhenius form to obtain  $E_{m P}$ . These  $E_{m P}$  are plotted in Figure 2b along with data for neat PMA (nPMA) as a function of  $d_{m k}^2$ . First, note that the results appear to be independent of  $M_{m n}$ . For small penetrants ( $d_{m k}^2 < 11~{\rm Å}^2$ ), the GNP systems track the neat polymer data, and thus, the  $E_{m P}$  varies linearly with  $d_{m k}^2$ . For larger gases ( $d_{m k}^2 > 11~{\rm Å}^2$ ), the activation energy in the GNP appears to plateau. The plateau for larger penetrants indicates that above a critical gas size, the energy barrier for permeability in GNPs is unaffected by gas size. These data are consistent, to within a factor of 2, with previously reported values for the activation energy of segmental chain motion, thus reinforcing the idea that penetrant transport in these materials is controlled by local chain dynamics.
discussion: GNPs as Heterogeneous Media. We focus the remainder of this discussion on a unifying model that rationalizes the penetrant size dependence for activation energies and solubilities. To this end, we first refer the reader to our previous work outlining a multi-body schematic representation for the underlying structure of GNPs, Figure 4a. Within that framework, polymer chains grafted to the particle surface comprise two regimes: (1) a dry zone of higher polymer stretching closer to the NP surface (referred to hereafter as the proximal region) and (2) the interstitial spaces in the multibody packing which should have a lower monomer density. We therefore propose a model by which larger penetrants are preferentially solubilized in the interstitial region. To transport from one interstitial pocket to another, they must navigate the more highly stretched proximal chain regions, effectively performing short jumps between adjacent pockets via a stretched-chain bridge (Figure 4b). Smaller penetrants, which are present in larger quantities, on the other hand dissolve throughout the entire medium, and hence, they do not perform these hop-like motions. This model also helps explain the more significant enhancements observed in the permeability of larger penetrants in GNPs relative to that in the neat melt. Lower solubility gases only require access to the relatively lower density interstitial regions; this process is less energy-intensive than corresponding sorption in the neat melt. Light gases like He and H2, despite having lower solubilities (assumed to be proportional to their lower  $T_{m b}$ ), are likely too small in this case to be size-limited in their transport and will also disperse throughout the entire system.
conclusion: This work sets out to identify and elaborate on the greater transport enhancement that larger penetrants exhibit in highly grafted GNP systems relative to smaller gases. Critically, we have demonstrated an ability to infer mechanisms for microscopic transport phenomena through direct macroscopic experimentation. We determined that the energy barrier for permeability in these systems is lower than in the neat polymer melt and that it plateaus for larger penetrant sizes. This finding has been rationalized through a model where gases larger than a critical size preferentially transport through the interstitial regions via a hopping mechanism, utilizing adjacent dry zones as a bridge from one interstitial pocket to another. The solubility enhancement, and corresponding reduction in solubility enthalpy, of larger gases further supports this transport picture. We now believe to have identified the primary pathway for large  $(d_k^2 > 11 	ext{ Å}^2)$  penetrant transport in GNP systems. We also propose a mechanism for smaller gas transport in these materials. Data shows a slight decrease in small penetrant activation energy  $(E_P)$  relative to the neat, as well as a slight decrease in their solubility and increase in their sorption enthalpy. Combined, this indicates that enhancements in smaller gas permeability, and the corresponding reduction in the energy barrier, are due to significant enhancements in the penetrant diffusion, consistent with our previous hypotheses.
